545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).
626	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient
637	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) can be recovered from
638	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}
678	
679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}
733	
734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}

793	
794	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
795	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
796	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
797	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These

803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}

834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of

844	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1).
845	\end{theorem}

847	\begin{definition}\leanok

855	  \emph{tensor-presentation iso} packages the canonical iso

865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the

874	[\(\Sigma\)-pair packaging: section-level LinearEquiv + intertwining]
875	  \label{def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma}

880	  Iter-188 Lane F-introduced \(\Sigma\)-pair packaging declaration that

986	\begin{lemma}
987	
988	\leanok
989	[Quasi-coherent module on an affine open is \texttt{tilde} of its sections (Stacks 01I8)]
990	  \label{lem:tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen}
992	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
993	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01I8}{01I8}
994	  (a quasi-coherent module on an affine scheme is \(\widetilde M\) for
995	  some module).}
996	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal N\) a quasi-coherent
997	  \(\mathcal O_X\)-module, and \(V \subset X\) an affine open. Then the
998	  pullback of \(\mathcal N\) along \texttt{hV.fromSpec} is canonically
999	  isomorphic to \texttt{tilde} of its sections:
1000	  \[
1001	    (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,\mathtt{hV.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
1002	      \mathcal N
1003	    \;\cong\;
1004	    \widetilde{\,\Gamma(\mathcal N, V)\,}
1005	    \quad\text{(on \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\Gamma(X, V)\)).}
1006	  \]
1007	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} ships the analog for \emph{free}
1008	  quasi-coherent modules (\texttt{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}
1009	  partial), but the general statement
1010	  ``\([N.IsQuasicoherent] \Rightarrow N|_V \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(N,V)}\)
1011	  on an affine open \(V\)'' (Stacks 01I8 \emph{lemma-affine-qc-module-iff})
1012	  is unowned at the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} layer.

1034	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict}

1036	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke transport, formed from
1037	  Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH} +
1038	  \texttt{tilde.isoTop}.}
1039	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal N\) an \(\mathcal O_Y\)-module, and
1040	  \(U \subset Y\) an affine open. Equip \(\Gamma((\mathrm{Spec}\,\Gamma(Y, U)),
1041	  \top)\) with the \(\Gamma(Y, U)\)-algebra structure inherited from
1042	  \texttt{Scheme.ΓSpecIso} (the canonical
1043	  \(\Gamma(\mathrm{Spec}\,R, \top) \cong R\) for any commutative ring \(R\)),
1044	  and equip
1045	  \(\Gamma((\text{pullback}\,\mathtt{hU.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal N,
1046	  \top)\) with the \(\Gamma(Y, U)\)-module structure inherited via
1047	  \texttt{Module.compHom}. Then there is a canonical \(\Gamma(Y, U)\)-linear
1048	  isomorphism between this pullback-section module and the original section
1049	  module \(\Gamma(\mathcal N, U)\):
1050	  \[
1051	    \Gamma((\text{pullback}\,\mathtt{hU.fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal N,
1052	      \top)
1053	    \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y, U)}\;
1054	    \Gamma(\mathcal N, U).
1055	  \]
1056	  The map identifies a section of the pulled-back sheaf at \(\top\) of
1057	  \(\mathrm{Spec}\,\Gamma(Y, U)\) with the corresponding section of
1058	  \(\mathcal N\) on the affine open \(U \subset Y\). This pin is the
1059	  iter-190+ prover target as Step 3 of the iter-189 \(\Sigma\)-pair
1060	  unbundle.
1061	\end{lemma}

1078	\section{Lean encoding}
1079	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
1080	
1081	The Lean target file is the new
1082	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
1083	proceeds in three phases.
1084	
1085	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
1086	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
1087	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
1088	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
1089	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
1090	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
1091	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
1092	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
1093	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
1094	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
1095	
1096	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
1097	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
1098	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
1099	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
1100	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
1101	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
1102	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
1103	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
1104	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
1105	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
1106	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
1107	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
1108	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
1109	
1110	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
1111	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1112	Grassmannian
1113	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
1114	\begin{enumerate}
1115	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
1116	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
1117	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
1118	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
1119	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
1120	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
1121	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
1122	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
1123	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
1124	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
1125	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
1126	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
1127	\end{enumerate}
1128	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
1129	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
1130	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
1131	
1132	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
1133	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
1134	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
1135	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
1136	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
1137	pinned commit.
1138	
1139	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
1140	This file directly consumes
1141	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
1142	gluing infrastructure and
1143	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
1144	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
1145	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
1146	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
1147	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
1148	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
1149	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
1150	
1151	\section{Out of scope}
1152	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
1153	
1154	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
1155	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
1156	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1157	\begin{itemize}
1158	  \item \textbf{Flattening stratification} (A.2.a) -- consumed-by, not
1159	    proved-here. Its blueprint home is the new chapter
1160	    \texttt{Picard\_FlatteningStratification.tex}.
1161	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1162	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1163	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot scheme and is
1164	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
1165	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford \(m\)-regularity theorem} -- input to
1166	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}, not proved here. The proof lives in
1167	    Nitsure \S 2; for the Lean encoding it is a project-side sub-build.
1168	  \item \textbf{Semi-continuity and base change} (Nitsure \S 3) -- the
1169	    flat-base-change theorem of cohomology (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology})
1170	    is stated here as a lemma, but the upstream Mathlib bridge / project-side
1171	    sub-build is not constructed in this chapter.
1172	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
1173	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
1174	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
1175	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
1176	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
1177	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
1178	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
1179	  \item \textbf{Hironaka's counterexample to representability of
1180	    \(\Hilb_{X/\mathbb{C}}\) in the non-projective case} (Nitsure \S 1,
1181	    Exercise (8)) -- pedagogical only; Route A operates entirely in the
1182	    projective setting.
1183	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
1184	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream of the Quot existence
1185	    statement; its blueprint home is
1186	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
1187	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
1188	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
1189	    constructed in this chapter.
1190	\end{itemize}

12	## Question
13	
14	(from iter-189 directive) Three sub-questions:
15	
16	1. Is `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` the correct Mathlib API path for proving
17	   `IsBaseChange e (pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap g N e)` at Lane F, or
18	   should the project switch to `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique` or a
19	   different constructor?
20	2. What is the cleanest Mathlib-aligned recipe for the `Nonempty Σ-pair`
21	   signature of `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`?
22	3. What are the Mathlib lemmas behind each of the 5 steps of the Tilde-
23	   isoTop route (Steps 1 = `N|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N,V)`; 2 = pullback-tildeIso;
24	   3 = `hU.isoSpec` transport; 4 = `tilde.isoTop`; 5 = adjunction-unit
25	   naturality)?

45	## Mathlib precedents examined (b80f227)
46

48	|---|---|---|

65	## Decisions identified
66	
67	### Decision 1: `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` vs `of_lift_unique` vs `ofEquiv`
68	
69	- **Mathlib idiom**: For "I have an explicit equiv `S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N`
70	  and a 1-tensor intertwining property", the canonical constructor is
71	  `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` (`IsTensorProduct.lean:394`). The 1-tensor
72	  intertwining `∀ x, e (1 ⊗ₜ x) = f x` is exactly the data needed.
73	
74	  `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique` is for the *universal-property* path:
75	  you must produce, for every target `Q`, a unique extension of every
76	  `R`-linear `M → Q` to an `S`-linear `N → Q`. That is structurally
77	  harder to discharge when the equiv is constructed concretely on the
78	  Spec side.
79	
80	  `IsBaseChange.ofEquiv` (capital E) only handles `e : M ≃ₗ[R] N`
81	  (same scalar ring) — gives `IsBaseChange R e.toLinearMap`. Inapplicable
82	  to Lane F's `R = Γ(X,V) → S = Γ(Y,U)` setup.
83	
84	- **Project's current path** (`QuotScheme.lean:672-695`): produces a
85	  `Nonempty {f : Γ(X,V) ⊗ Γ(N,V) ≃ₗ[Γ(Y,U)] Γ((pullback g).obj N, U) //
86	   ∀ x, f (1 ⊗ₜ x) = pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap g N e x}` via the
87	  named helper, then dispatches `IsBaseChange.of_equiv equiv hApp`.
88	
89	- **Gap**: identical. The project's path is exactly the Mathlib idiom.
90	
91	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** — `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` is the correct API
92	  path. The other constructors are structurally heavier or inapplicable.
93	
94	### Decision 2: `Nonempty Σ-pair` packaging idiom
95	
96	- **Mathlib idiom**: Mathlib has no `LinearEquiv.intertwines` predicate.
97	  The closest idioms are:
98	  (a) Return data + Prop as TWO declarations (a `noncomputable def`
99	      producing the equiv, a `theorem` recording the intertwining).
100	  (b) Return `∃ f : LinearEquiv ..., ∀ x, ...` (a Prop with a Type-valued
101	      witness — perfectly legal since `LinearEquiv` is a `Type`, not
102	      `Prop`).
103	  (c) Return `Nonempty {f : LinearEquiv // ∀ x, ...}` — the project's
104	      current choice. Definitionally a heavier wrapping of (b).
105	
106	- **Project's current path**: option (c). Consumer in
107	  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_isBaseChange` (L691) unpacks via
108	  `obtain ⟨equiv, hApp⟩ := ...` then feeds into `IsBaseChange.of_equiv`.
109	
110	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent. (c) and (b) are interchangeable; (c)
111	  is mildly noisier (an extra `Subtype.mk` layer when constructing).
112	  Mathlib leans toward (b) for pure-existence statements (`∃ e, ...`).
113	  Once the helper body is closed, the natural endpoint is *not* (b)
114	  or (c) but (a): a `noncomputable def` for the equiv + a `simp` lemma
115	  for `(1 ⊗ₜ x) = baseMap x`. But while the body is a typed sorry,
116	  packaging as `Nonempty` (which only asserts inhabitedness, not
117	  computability) is the right hygiene.
118	
119	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** with current `Nonempty {f // ...}` packaging.
120	  Optionally consider switching to bare `∃ f, ...` for syntactic
121	  parsimony (saves ~1 LOC in consumer obtain pattern). NOT a blocker.
122	
123	### Decision 3: 5-step Tilde-isoTop Mathlib lemmas per step
124	
125	| Step | Project description | Mathlib lemma(s) at b80f227 | Status |
126	|---|---|---|---|
127	| 1 | `N\|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N, V)` on `Spec Γ(X, V)` | Composition: `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff` (`Tilde.lean:340`) ↔ `essImage tilde`; OR `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation` (`Tilde.lean:398`) from a `Presentation`. The "extract a presentation of `(N\|_V).overSpec` from `[N.IsQuasicoherent]`" sub-step is a **Mathlib gap** (Stacks 01I8: QC ⟺ tilde on every affine open). `IsQuasicoherent` only supplies a cover + presentations on COVER elements (`Quasicoherent.lean:208`), not on a chosen affine open `V`. | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL |
128	| 2 | Pullback via `Spec.map φ`: `(pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M) ≅ tilde (B ⊗_A M)` | None at b80f227. Direct LSP searches (`pullback.*tilde`, `tilde.*pullback`, `baseChange.*tilde`) return zero matches. Project pin: `pullback_tildeIso` (Stacks 01HQ / 0BJ8). | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (project pin in place) |
129	| 3 | Transport via `hU.isoSpec` back to `U`-sections | Combines `IsAffineOpen.isoSpec` (`AffineScheme.lean:380`), `IsAffineOpen.fromSpec` (`AffineScheme.lean:414`), and "module-pullback along an open immersion is restriction" — the LAST piece is a **Mathlib gap**: there is no `Scheme.Modules.pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restriction` lemma at b80f227. The conceptual content is that `Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι ≅ Scheme.Modules.restrictionToOpen U`. | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (no project pin yet) |
130	| 4 | Evaluate at `⊤` via `tilde.isoTop` | `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.isoTop` (`Tilde.lean:177`). **MATHLIB HAS.** | OK |
131	| 5 | Naturality of adjunction unit (1-tensor intertwining) | `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction` (`Tilde.lean:279`) + `IsIso (tilde.adjunction.unit)` instance (`Tilde.lean:307`). The specific compatibility with `pullback_app_isoTensor_unitAtV` is project-side glue (~20-40 LOC). | OK (Mathlib supplies the adjunction; specific intertwining is project glue) |
132	
133	- **Verdict per step**:
134	  - Step 4: **PROCEED** — Mathlib lemma in place.
135	  - Step 5: **PROCEED** — Mathlib adjunction in place; intertwining is
136	    project glue (no Mathlib gap; just naturality bookkeeping).
137	  - Steps 1, 2, 3: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**. Of these, Step 2 has a
138	    project pin (`pullback_tildeIso`). Steps 1 and 3 currently do NOT
139	    have separate pins — their substantive content is bundled into the
140	    body of `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`.
141	
142	### Decision 4: Pin Steps 1 and 3 as separate typed sorries (refactor)
143	
144	- **Mathlib idiom**: Mathlib's pattern for substantive missing
145	  infrastructure is one named declaration per Stacks tag, so future
146	  porting-to-Mathlib lands one PR per tag.
147	
148	- **Project's current path**: All three Mathlib gaps (Steps 1, 2, 3)
149	  are conceptually independent, but only Step 2 is split out. Steps 1
150	  and 3 are buried inside `_sectionLinearEquiv`'s body sorry, making
151	  the `K=4 iters / 9→11 sorry creep / 6 helpers / PARTIAL×4` STUCK
152	  pattern (per iter-188 progress-critic) hard to break: the prover
153	  cannot make progress on `_sectionLinearEquiv` because closing it
154	  requires *three* substantive Stacks lemmas, only ONE of which has a
155	  name to put in scope.
156	
157	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. Cost: provers iterate on the bundled
158	  helper without a way to localize which Stacks gap is the residual
159	  obstruction; the typed-sorry budget keeps growing without progress.
160	
161	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — split out Step 1 and Step 3 as
162	  separately-named typed-sorry pins, parallel to `pullback_tildeIso`
163	  for Step 2. After this refactor, the body of `_sectionLinearEquiv`
164	  becomes pure compositional glue with no internal Mathlib gaps, and
165	  prover work can focus on the three named pins independently.
166	
167	## Recommendation
168	
169	**iter-189 blueprint + prover dispatch for Lane F**:
170	
171	1. **Confirm**: the `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` API path taken in iter-188
172	   is correct. No pivot needed. The structural choice is the right
173	   Mathlib idiom. (Decisions 1, 2.)
174	
175	2. **Refactor (high priority)**: split out two new typed-sorry pins
176	   in `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`, parallel to the existing
177	   `pullback_tildeIso`:
178	
179	   - `tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen` (Step 1):
180	     given `[N.IsQuasicoherent]` and `hV : IsAffineOpen V`, produce
181	     `Nonempty (N|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N, V) /* on Spec Γ(X, V) */)`.
182	     Stacks 01I8 content. ~20-40 LOC body.
183	
184	   - `pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict` (Step 3):
185	     given an open immersion `j : U ⟶ X` (specifically `U.ι`), produce
186	     `Nonempty (Scheme.Modules.pullback j ≅ Scheme.Modules.restrictionToOpen U)`,
187	     or equivalently a section-level naturality lemma packaging the
188	     `hU.isoSpec` transport. Stacks gap. ~30-50 LOC body.
189	
190	3. **After the refactor**, the body of `_sectionLinearEquiv` becomes
191	   pure glue: chain Step 1 + Step 2 (`pullback_tildeIso`) + Step 3
192	   transport + Step 4 (`tilde.isoTop`) + Step 5 (naturality), using
193	   `LinearEquiv.trans`. Estimated ~50-80 LOC of glue. The single typed
194	   sorry on `_sectionLinearEquiv` then evaporates — its body closes
195	   axiom-clean given the three named-pin Mathlib gaps.
196	
197	4. **Blueprint expansion** (`Picard_QuotScheme.tex`): add Lean-4-
198	   friendly decomposition matching the three named pins. The blueprint
199	   currently has `def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma` as a plan-phase pin;
200	   expand it to explicitly enumerate the three Mathlib-gap steps and
201	   the two Mathlib-supplied steps (4 & 5).
202	
203	5. **Do NOT** switch to `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique`. The universal-
204	   property hypothesis would require the same Mathlib gaps (no shortcut)
205	   AND introduce an extra layer of `∃!` bookkeeping. The project's
206	   `of_equiv` path is strictly cleaner.
207	
208	## Verdict summary
209	
210	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
211	|---|---|---|
212	| 1. `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` API path | PROCEED (identical to Mathlib idiom) | informational |
213	| 2. `Nonempty Σ-pair` packaging | PROCEED (divergent-equivalent; bare `∃ f, ...` slightly cleaner but not a blocker) | informational |
214	| 3. 5-step Tilde route — Steps 4, 5 | PROCEED (Mathlib supplies the lemmas) | informational |
215	| 3. 5-step Tilde route — Steps 1, 2, 3 | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (Step 2 has a project pin; Steps 1 & 3 do NOT) | high |
216	| 4. Pin Steps 1 and 3 separately | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (refactor) | critical (unbundles the STUCK helper) |
217	
218	**Overall verdict**: **(A) STRUCTURAL OK** with a critical refactor
219	recommendation. The `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` choice is structurally
220	correct and does NOT need a pivot. The reason iter-188's helper has
221	been STUCK across 4 iters is *not* because the API path is wrong, but
222	because three independent Mathlib gaps are bundled into one named
223	helper, leaving provers without separate targets. Pinning Steps 1 and

    (algInst : Algebra R Γ(Spec R, ⊤))
    [Module R other] (hsmul : ∀ (r : R) (x : other), h (r • x) = r • h x) :

import Mathlib
open CategoryTheory AlgebraicGeometry

-- Test: is `Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` already an R-module by inference?
noncomputable example (R : CommRingCat) (M : ModuleCat R) :
    Module R Γ(tilde M, ⊤) := inferInstance

import Mathlib
open CategoryTheory AlgebraicGeometry

-- The smul compatibility I need
noncomputable example (R : CommRingCat) (M N : (Spec R).Modules) (φ : M ⟶ N) :
    letI : Algebra R Γ(Spec R, ⊤) := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom.toAlgebra
    letI : Module R Γ(M, ⊤) :=
      Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
    letI : Module R Γ(N, ⊤) :=
      Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
    ∀ (r : R) (x : Γ(M, ⊤)),
      (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom (r • x) = r • (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom x := by
  letI algInst : Algebra R Γ(Spec R, ⊤) := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom.toAlgebra
  letI modM : Module R Γ(M, ⊤) :=
    Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
  letI modN : Module R Γ(N, ⊤) :=
    Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
  intro r x
  -- LHS: φ_⊤ ((ΓSpecIso.inv r) • x) -- Module.compHom
  -- RHS: (ΓSpecIso.inv r) • φ_⊤ x -- Module.compHom
  change (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom r • x) =
         (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom r • (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom x
  exact Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul φ _ x

# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean — iter-194 prover

## Lane F: LinearEquiv extraction in `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`

### Result: PARTIAL (structural progress; sorry count unchanged at 12)

- **LinearEquiv portion**: axiom-clean.
- **Beck-Chevalley intertwining**: typed sorry (architecturally blocked
  on `step1` + `step2` bodies).

### Attempt 1 (line 815: `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`)
- **Approach**: Build `f : T ≃ₗ[Γ(Y,U)] Γ((pullback g).obj N, U)` from
  the existing `composedIso` chain by:
  1. Introducing local alias `TR := ModuleCat.of Γ(Y,U) (T)` to silence
     `Γ(·,·)` notation ambiguity inside the tilde type ascription.
  2. Restating the `Module.compHom`-via-`ΓSpecIso.inv.hom` instance for
     the source of `topAdd` so that `topAdd.toLinearEquiv` typechecks
     (the source-side instance from `step3`'s signature did not carry
     through `obtain ⟨step3⟩` to the calling scope).
  3. `topLin = topAdd.toLinearEquiv ...` with the smul-compat proof
     reducing to `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul composedIso.hom`.
  4. `toTensor := topLin.trans (tilde.isoTop TR).symm.toLinearEquiv`
     (the underlying types `Γ(tilde TR, ⊤)` and `(modulesSpecToSheaf.obj
     (tilde TR)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)` are defeq; the Γ(Y,U)-module
     structures match via `Module.compHom` vs. `restrictScalars
     globalSectionsIso.hom`).
  5. `f := toTensor.symm.trans step3`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED for steps 1-5 (LinearEquiv construction axiom-clean).
- **Key insight**: `(tilde.isoTop M).toLinearEquiv` directly produces the
  needed `M ≃ₗ[R] Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` (the two Γ(Y,U)-module instances are
  defeq enough to make this typecheck without a manual `Module.compHom`
  bridge on the tilde side).

### Attempt 2 (line 957: Beck-Chevalley intertwining at `1 ⊗ₜ x`)
- **Approach**: Tried `rfl`, `simp [f, toTensor, topLin, topAdd,
  composedIso, pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap, ...]`, `unfold ...`.
- **Result**: FAILED (`rfl` unfolds the goal to a chain involving
  `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app step1.inv`, `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app step2.inv`,
  and `(tilde.isoTop TR).symm.toLinearEquiv.symm` applied to `1 ⊗ₜ x`,
  which cannot be closed by `rfl`/`simp` alone).
- **Architectural finding**: The intertwining provably DEPENDS on the
  bodies of `step1` (`tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen`,
  Stacks 01I8) and `step2` (`pullback_tildeIso`, Stacks 01HQ). Both are
  currently `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` typed-sorry pins; their isos are
  opaque (Classical.choice-extracted), so the LHS chain cannot be
  reduced to a closed form. **The intertwining is unprovable until
  either**:
  - (a) `step1`'s and `step2`'s bodies land (Mathlib gaps Stacks 01I8 +
    01HQ), OR
  - (b) `step1` and `step2` signatures are refactored to Σ-pair form
    carrying the canonical iso-characterizing identity (e.g.,
    `step1.hom = (asIso fromTildeΓ).inv` for QC-on-affine, or
    `step2.hom = canonical tilde-pullback-iso` for Spec base change).

### Mathlib precedent searches done
- `IsQuasicoherent.pushforward`: no direct API at pinned commit (Stacks
  01XJ unowned at `Scheme.Modules` layer per analogist verdict in
  `analogies/lane-f-isbasechange.md`).
- `Module.compHom` + `restrictScalars`: defeq verified via
  `lean_run_code` test (the Γ(Y,U)-module on `Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` via
  `Module.compHom _ ΓSpecIso.inv.hom` matches the natural one via
  `(modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)`).
- `tilde.isoTop`: confirmed at `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Tilde.lean:177`,
  produces the canonical `M ≅ (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)`.
- `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul`: confirmed at
  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean:110`, gives
  Γ(X,U)-linearity of `Hom.app φ U`.

### Status post-iter-194

- **Sorry count**: 12 (unchanged from iter-193).
- **Structural**: `_sectionLinearEquiv` body is now ~30 LOC of pure
  compositional glue (axiom-clean LinearEquiv construction) followed by
  ONE typed sorry on the intertwining. Pre-iter-194, the entire
  `Nonempty {f // ...}` was a single typed sorry. Post-iter-194, the
  LinearEquiv `f` is concretely built and the residual sorry is a
  focused identity `f (1 ⊗ₜ x) = baseMap g N e x`.

### Suggested iter-195+ next steps for the Plan agent

1. **Refactor step1/step2 to Σ-pair signatures** with canonical-iso
   identities. This is the unblocking refactor for `_sectionLinearEquiv`
   body closure. Cost: ~10-15 LOC per pin (the signature gains a
   `// step1.hom = canonical_map`-style clause; the bodies still typed
   sorry but the consumer can use the identity).
2. **OR**: Close `step1`'s body via Stacks 01I8 path (extract a
   Presentation of `(N|_V).overSpec` from `[N.IsQuasicoherent] + hV`
   transport, then apply `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation`). Estimated
   ~50-80 LOC; Mathlib gap is the per-affine-open presentation
   extraction (`QuasicoherentData.presentation` only ships per-cover
   element).
3. Either path unblocks the Beck-Chevalley intertwining and closes
   `_sectionLinearEquiv` axiom-clean, which then propagates to
   `pullback_app_isoTensor_isBaseChange` and `Scheme.Modules.pullback_app_isoTensor`.

### HARD BAR status

iter-194 HARD BAR was "≥1 axiom-clean closure". This was NOT met
(no full closure of any typed sorry); however, **substantial structural
progress** landed:
- The LinearEquiv extraction (Steps a-c of the iter-189 5-step plan)
  is now axiom-clean.
- The body of `_sectionLinearEquiv` no longer hides three Mathlib gaps
  + bookkeeping under one bare sorry; the sorry is now narrowed to the
  Beck-Chevalley intertwining at `1 ⊗ₜ x` (a single specific identity).
- An architectural finding was surfaced: the intertwining is provably
  blocked on step1/step2 body landings OR signature strengthening —
  this is concrete guidance for iter-195+ plan-phase routing.

PUSH-BEYOND (full `_sectionLinearEquiv` close) was NOT achievable this
iter without prior step1/step2 work.

## iter-194 · parallel prover

Lane F's `_sectionLinearEquiv` body sorry is structurally blocked
because its dependency pins (`tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen`,
`pullback_tildeIso`) have `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` signatures. The
opaque `.choice`-extracted iso means the body's intertwining check
(Beck-Chevalley at `1 ⊗ₜ x`) cannot be computed even when the
LinearEquiv portion is axiom-clean. Each prover iter on Lane F
re-derives this blocker and burns budget pretending closure is
incrementally possible.

Suggestion: an automatable "pin-strength audit" subagent could flag
typed-sorry pins whose signatures are too weak to enable consumer
closure (i.e., `Nonempty` carrying no characterizing identity). The
plan agent could then route either to "close pin body" OR "strengthen
pin signature" iter-N+1, instead of repeatedly dispatching consumer
provers who hit the same architectural wall.

EOF
